findings.summary: temper_request and temper_governance_request are nearly identical functions with duplicated response handling logic
findings.evidence: temper_request (lines 13-63) and temper_governance_request (lines 66-113) share identical response body parsing, empty-body handling, authz denied formatting, and error formatting logic. temper_request_bytes (lines 116-158) also duplicates the response parsing. The only differences are in header construction (principal kind and default principal ID).
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared response-processing helper (e.g. parse_temper_response) and a request-builder that accepts a header configuration enum or struct. Each public function would configure headers then delegate to the shared core.

findings.summary: persisted_status_to_registry_status and turso_status_to_registry_status are structurally identical functions with trivial differences
findings.evidence: persisted_status_to_registry_status (lines 130-176) and turso_status_to_registry_status (lines 338-384) have identical control flow, identical match arms (pending/running/fallback), and identical level-reconstruction logic. The only material difference is the input row type (PersistedSpecRow vs TursoSpecRow) and the verification_result field type (serde_json::Value vs Option<String>). Similarly, load_registry_from_postgres and load_registry_from_turso (lines 178-335) duplicate the entire grouping-by-tenant, CSDL parsing, and registration logic.
findings.suggestion: Define a trait (e.g. SpecRowLike) with accessors for the common fields, then implement it for both row types. Write a single generic load_registry_from_rows function and a single status_to_registry_status function parameterized over the trait.

findings.summary: temper-evolution RecordStore is explicitly deprecated but still used and has HashMap instead of BTreeMap
findings.evidence: The module doc (line 7) states: 'Deprecated: Evolution records are now managed as IOA entities in the temper-system tenant (ADR-0025). This store is retained for backward compatibility during migration.' Yet it uses HashMap (line 11) instead of BTreeMap, violating the codebase deterministic simulation convention. The store is also still cloneable and exported via pub use in lib.rs (line 27). No deprecation attributes are applied to the type or its methods.
findings.suggestion: Either remove the deprecated RecordStore entirely if migration to IOA entities is complete, or apply #[deprecated] attributes to its public API and switch HashMap to BTreeMap for DST compliance while it remains.

findings.summary: RecordStore has five identical insert_X / get_X method pairs and save_to_directory repeats the same 5-line block five times
findings.evidence: insert_observation, insert_problem, insert_analysis, insert_decision, insert_insight (lines 50-92) are structurally identical: each acquires a write lock, then inserts into the appropriate HashMap. get_observation through get_insight (lines 97-119) are also identical. save_to_directory (lines 168-212) repeats the pattern create_dir_all -> iterate HashMap -> serialize -> write for each of the 5 record types. This is classic AI-generated boilerplate that could be data-driven.
findings.suggestion: Use a macro or a generic helper method that operates on a (sub_dir_name, HashMap) pair. For save_to_directory, extract a save_records_to_subdir<T: Serialize> helper. For the insert/get pairs, consider a generic record store backed by a BTreeMap<RecordId, Box<dyn Any>> with type-safe wrappers, or a macro that generates the methods.

findings.summary: insight_generator.rs mixes three distinct pipeline stages (aggregation, cross-referencing, insight generation) with repeated InsightRecord construction boilerplate
findings.evidence: generate_insights (lines 35-248) contains three separate concern blocks: signal aggregation (lines 41-73), cross-referencing (lines 76-80), and insight generation (lines 85-239). The insight generation block has three different special cases (EntitySetNotFound at line 98, authz denials at line 150, general at line 183) each of which constructs an InsightSignal + InsightRecord with the same boilerplate. generate_unmet_intents (lines 254-324) duplicates some of the same aggregation/categorization logic. generate_feature_requests (lines 349-415) is a third variation with similar accumulation patterns.
findings.suggestion: Extract the InsightRecord construction into a builder or factory method. The three generation functions share the same accumulation pattern (group by key -> filter by threshold -> construct record); consider a trait or generic pipeline that encapsulates this pattern.

findings.summary: Observe API endpoints return inconsistent response shapes: some wrap in {items, total}, others return bare arrays
findings.evidence: list_agents returns {agents, total} (line 104). list_entities returns a bare Vec<EntityInstanceSummary> (line 23). list_evolution_records returns {records, total_observations, ...} (line 57). handle_feature_requests returns a bare JSON array (line 267). list_evolution_insights returns {insights, total} (line 112). get_agent_history returns {agent_id, history, total} (line 159). These sibling endpoints under /observe have no consistent envelope pattern.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on a single response envelope for collection endpoints, e.g. {items: [...], total: N, ...optional_metadata}. Apply this consistently across all observe API endpoints.

findings.summary: BlockingWasmDispatch struct is a thin wrapper that immediately destructures into 8 positional arguments
findings.evidence: BlockingWasmDispatch (lines 13-22) wraps 8 borrowed fields. dispatch_wasm_integrations_blocking (lines 31-51) immediately destructures it into positional arguments passed to dispatch_wasm_integrations_internal. The struct exists only to satisfy the too_many_arguments clippy lint rather than adding genuine domain encapsulation. The internal method still takes 9 positional parameters.
findings.suggestion: If the struct is kept, have dispatch_wasm_integrations_internal accept it directly instead of destructuring back to positional args. Alternatively, define a shared WasmDispatchRequest that both blocking and non-blocking paths use natively.

findings.summary: temper-server depends on 12 internal crates, making it a coupling hub with high blast radius
findings.evidence: temper-server/Cargo.toml lists dependencies on temper-runtime, temper-odata, temper-spec, temper-jit, temper-store-postgres, temper-store-turso, temper-store-redis, temper-observe, temper-authz, temper-evolution, temper-wasm, and temper-mcp. This means any change to any of these 12 crates triggers a recompile of temper-server. The 'observe' feature gate exists for temper-verify but the others are unconditional.
findings.suggestion: Consider whether temper-mcp and temper-wasm could be behind feature gates or split into separate server extension crates. The store backends (postgres, turso, redis) could potentially be abstracted behind a single store trait to reduce the direct dependency count.

findings.summary: Inconsistent error mapping: some code uses .context() (anyhow), others use .map_err(|e| anyhow::anyhow!(...)), and HTTP handlers use bare String errors
findings.evidence: In storage.rs, connect_postgres_store uses .context() (line 45) alongside .map_err(|e| anyhow::anyhow!(...)) (lines 48, 53) for the same function's error handling. In http.rs, temper_request returns Result<Value, String> (line 21) using format!() for errors. In dispatch/actions.rs, dispatch_tenant_action returns Result<EntityResponse, String>. The codebase has three distinct error strategies: anyhow in CLI, String in server dispatch/HTTP, and thiserror in library crates (McpError, ObserveError).
findings.suggestion: The layered error strategy (thiserror in libraries, anyhow in CLI) is reasonable, but the String error returns in temper-server dispatch functions lose type information. Consider a ServerDispatchError enum for the dispatch layer, converting to String only at the HTTP boundary.

findings.summary: Observe endpoints silently degrade to empty responses when Turso is unavailable, with no indication to the caller
findings.evidence: In list_agents (lines 81-119), if turso_opt() returns None or the query fails, the endpoint returns {agents: [], total: 0} with HTTP 200. The caller receives the same response shape whether no agents exist or the backend is unavailable. The tracing::warn on failure (line 110) is only visible in server logs. The same pattern occurs in list_evolution_records (lines 19-81), list_evolution_insights (lines 85-128), and handle_feature_requests.
findings.suggestion: Add a metadata field to fallback responses indicating data_source: 'unavailable' or return HTTP 503 when the backing store is not configured. Alternatively, include a boolean field like 'data_available': false in the fallback path.

findings.summary: Authorization denial recording mixes eprintln! and tracing for error reporting
findings.evidence: record_authz_denial (line 50) uses eprintln! for failed decision persistence (line 87: 'Warning: failed to persist pending decision') and failed trajectory persistence (line 141: 'Warning: failed to persist authz trajectory'), while using tracing::warn for the entity creation failure (line 114). This is inconsistent: all three are the same category of non-fatal persistence failure in the same function.
findings.suggestion: Replace eprintln! with tracing::warn for consistency. The tracing framework provides structured logging with context, which is already used elsewhere in the same function.

findings.summary: handle_sentinel_check combines sentinel evaluation, insight generation, Turso persistence, entity creation, and response formatting in a single 160-line function
findings.evidence: handle_sentinel_check (lines 26-163) performs: (1) trajectory loading, (2) sentinel rule evaluation, (3) O-Record persistence to Turso, (4) Observation entity creation via dispatch, (5) result JSON construction, (6) insight generation, (7) insight persistence to Turso, (8) Insight entity creation via dispatch, (9) insight result JSON construction. handle_feature_requests (lines 186-277) similarly combines generation, upsert, entity creation, query, and formatting.
findings.suggestion: Extract persistence + entity creation into a helper (e.g. persist_and_create_entity). Split handle_sentinel_check into sentinel_check -> persist_alerts -> generate_and_persist_insights -> format_response. Each step should be a testable unit.

findings.summary: OData common helpers still fall back to legacy entity_set_map and csdl_xml fields on ServerState after SpecRegistry lookup
findings.evidence: resolve_entity_type (line 62-73) first queries registry.resolve_entity_type(), then falls back to state.entity_set_map. tenant_csdl_xml (line 78-86) falls back to state.csdl_xml. tenant_entity_sets (line 91-98) falls back to state.entity_set_map. These legacy fields on ServerState represent the pre-multi-tenant architecture. The fallbacks are always-present code paths that may mask registry configuration issues.
findings.suggestion: If all production deployments now use the SpecRegistry, remove the legacy fallbacks and the corresponding fields from ServerState. If backward compatibility is still needed, add deprecation warnings when fallback paths are hit to track migration progress.

findings.summary: turso_opt() method name does not communicate what it returns or why it might be None
findings.evidence: state.turso_opt() is called in at least 8 observe endpoints as the gate for whether Turso-backed queries are possible. The name 'turso_opt' is an implementation detail (returns an Option of a Turso store) rather than communicating intent (e.g. 'is persistent storage available'). Callers always pattern-match it with if let Some(turso) = state.turso_opt().
findings.suggestion: Consider renaming to persistent_store() or evolution_store() to communicate the domain concept rather than the implementation backend. This also makes future backend swaps transparent.

findings.summary: insight_generator.rs has no test module despite containing complex aggregation and classification logic
findings.evidence: insight_generator.rs (475 lines) contains generate_insights, generate_unmet_intents, generate_feature_requests, and categorize_error -- all pure functions that take trajectory entries and return records. These are ideal candidates for unit testing (no I/O, deterministic, complex edge cases like cross-referencing SubmitSpec events with EntitySetNotFound patterns). The file has no #[cfg(test)] module.
findings.suggestion: Add unit tests covering: (1) empty input, (2) signals below threshold (total < 2), (3) EntitySetNotFound with and without SubmitSpec cross-reference, (4) authz denial threshold boundary (>30%), (5) feature request generation below FEATURE_REQUEST_THRESHOLD, (6) priority sorting. These are all pure functions with no mocking needed.

findings.summary: Error handling diverges between anyhow::Result and custom ActorError enums without consistent patterns
findings.evidence: ActorError::Custom wraps anyhow via #[from], but dispatch code uses explicit Result<T, ActorError>. ServerState initialization uses env_bool() helper that silently defaults on parse errors instead of propagating errors like other modules do.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on either typed errors or anyhow throughout temper-server. Have env parsing fail fast instead of defaulting.

findings.summary: The observe feature pulls in temper-verify as a hard dependency, mixing production/test separation
findings.evidence: Line 11 of Cargo.toml: observe = ["temper-verify"]. This means any deployment using observe endpoints requires the full verification cascade at runtime.
findings.suggestion: Move temper-verify to dev-dependencies or split observe into a separate crate that only depends on verification outputs.

findings.summary: ServerState struct aggregates 25+ unrelated concerns (actor system, persistence, WASM, authz, reaction dispatch, webhooks, metrics)
findings.evidence: ServerState has fields for actor_system, csdl, event_store, registry, authz, reaction_dispatcher, webhook_dispatcher, wasm_engine, metrics, record_store, secrets_vault, entity_state_cache, eventual_tracker, idempotency_cache, etc. Multiple Arc<RwLock<T>> layers indicate unclear ownership boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Extract logical subsystems into smaller structs (ActorSystemService, PersistenceService, WasmService, AuthzService). Compose them into ServerState as nested fields.

findings.summary: ServerState::new() delegates to register_builtin_wasm_modules() which silently fails on compilation errors
findings.evidence: register_builtin_wasm_modules() is called during construction. Compilation failures are logged as warnings rather than propagating errors. This leaves the server in a degraded state without visibility to the caller.
findings.suggestion: Return Result from new() or make builtin module registration a separate post-construction step that must be explicitly called and checked.

findings.summary: resolve_value() uses hardcoded substring indices without explaining what {secret: length is
findings.evidence: Lines use &remaining[start + 8..] and remaining[start..start + 8 + end + 1] with magic number 8. The 8 is the length of "{secret:" but is not named or documented.
findings.suggestion: Define const PREFIX: &str = "{secret:" and use PREFIX.len() instead of hardcoded 8. Extract the pattern matching logic into a helper function.

findings.summary: WasmModuleRegistry uses String-keyed tuples (String, String) for (tenant, module_name) instead of a stronger type
findings.evidence: BTreeMap<(String, String), String> for (tenant, module_name). Callers convert TenantId to String repeatedly, losing type safety. Parameter order can be accidentally swapped.
findings.suggestion: Create struct ModuleKey { tenant: TenantId, name: String } or a newtype to ensure type-safe lookups.

findings.summary: temper-cli/src/serve/loader.rs combines spec loading, linting, and registry interaction without modular boundaries
findings.evidence: The file has three concerns: reading IOA/CSDL files, linting, and registry registration, all at module scope with shared error handling patterns that duplicate code from temper-server/observe/specs_helpers.rs.
findings.suggestion: Split into file I/O module, linting module shared with server via temper-spec, and registry integration.

findings.summary: WasmAuthzDecision::Deny(String) uses untyped String for structured denial reasons instead of an enum
findings.evidence: Deny(String) with tests hardcoding reason strings like "denied by policy". Real denials clone the denial reason. This prevents structured error handling and audit trails.
findings.suggestion: Change Deny(String) to Deny(DenialReason) enum with variants like PolicyViolation { policy_name: String }, ResourceNotAllowed { resource: String }.
